name: Patch Management Policy and Procedure
description: Written policy and procedure governing the patch management lifecycle — defining scan and identification frequency, severity-based remediation SLAs, patch testing requirements, deployment procedures, exception handling, and roles responsible for each phase.
reasoning: Direct evidence for SI.L1-3.14.1[a,c,e] — the requirement to define identification, reporting, and correction timeframes. Without documented SLAs by severity, assessors have no documented standard to measure actual remediation against. The policy is the first thing assessors request when evaluating SI.L1-3.14.1 — it establishes the intent and framework before operational evidence is reviewed.

name: Patch Deployment Records
description: Records of patches deployed to systems — including the CVEs or flaws addressed, the systems patched, the deployment date, and the person or process that applied the patch. May come from WSUS, SCCM, Intune, Ansible, or other patch management tools.
reasoning: Primary operational evidence for SI.L1-3.14.1[f] — that flaws are actually corrected within the specified timeframe. Assessors compare patch deployment dates against vulnerability identification dates to verify SLA compliance. Records showing critical patches deployed within the policy-defined window are the strongest evidence. Gaps between identification and deployment, or patches deployed to some systems but not others, are direct findings.

name: Endpoint Protection Coverage Report
description: Report from the endpoint protection management platform (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, Symantec, etc.) showing deployment coverage across all in-scope systems — enrollment status per device, last connection time, agent version, and signature currency.
reasoning: Covers three SI practices simultaneously. SI.L1-3.14.2[b] (protection deployed at designated locations) is satisfied by the coverage report showing all required systems enrolled. SI.L1-3.14.4[a] (updates current) is satisfied by the signature currency column. SI.L1-3.14.5[a,c] (periodic and real-time scanning active) is verified through the agent status showing on-access scanning enabled. Assessors request this report because it efficiently demonstrates all three practices from a single artifact.

name: Malware Detection and Response Records
description: Detection event logs, quarantine records, and incident tickets generated by endpoint protection, email security, and network security tools when malicious code is identified — showing what was detected, when, on which system, what action was taken, and disposition.
reasoning: Operational evidence that malicious code protection (SI.L1-3.14.2) is functioning in practice, not just deployed. SI.L1-3.14.5[b] explicitly requires identifying malicious code as a result of scans — detection records satisfy this. Assessors look for a reasonable volume of detection activity demonstrating the tools are active and catching things. Zero detections over a year may indicate detection is not functioning. High-severity detections should show incident response follow-through.

name: Security Alerts and Advisories Monitoring Records
description: Records documenting the monitoring and response to security alerts and advisories — subscription records for CISA/US-CERT alerts, NVD notifications, and vendor bulletins; advisory review logs; applicability assessments; and actions taken in response to applicable advisories.
reasoning: Direct evidence for all three objectives of SI.L2-3.14.3. Subscription records satisfy [b] (monitoring). Advisory review logs and applicability assessments satisfy [a] (response actions identified) and [c] (actions taken). Assessors look for a defined process connecting advisory receipt to action — not just that advisories arrive in an inbox, but that someone reviews them, assesses applicability, and either acts or documents why no action is needed.

name: Intrusion Detection and Prevention System Configuration
description: Configuration of network and host-based IDS/IPS tools — showing sensor placement at inbound and outbound network boundaries, detection rule sets or signatures deployed, alert thresholds, and log forwarding to SIEM. Includes network flow analysis tools if deployed.
reasoning: Primary technical evidence for SI.L2-3.14.6[a,b,c] — the deployment of monitoring capability at the system level and at inbound/outbound communications boundaries. Assessors verify sensor placement covers both directions and that detection rules are appropriate for the threat environment. An IDS with default rules only, or one that only covers inbound traffic, is a partial finding. The configuration demonstrates intent; detection event records demonstrate operation.

name: Network Monitoring and Detection Event Records
description: Event records from network monitoring tools — IDS/IPS alerts, firewall deny logs reviewed in SIEM, proxy logs showing blocked connections, DNS monitoring detections, and network traffic analysis anomaly alerts. Shows that monitoring is actively producing actionable output.
reasoning: Operational evidence that communications monitoring (SI.L2-3.14.6) is working. The configuration establishes the capability; the event records demonstrate it is running and producing output. Assessors ask for sample detection records to verify both inbound [b] and outbound [c] monitoring coverage. Outbound detection records are particularly valuable because they demonstrate the organization can identify C2 activity or exfiltration attempts — capabilities that many organizations lack.

name: Acceptable Use Policy
description: Written policy defining authorized use of organizational systems — covering authorized users, permitted data types and storage locations, authorized applications and services, permitted network connections, and expected behaviors. Should be specific enough to make unauthorized use detectable.
reasoning: Direct evidence for SI.L2-3.14.7[a] — that authorized use is defined. The Assessment Guide explicitly states 'define authorized use of your systems' as the prerequisite for identifying unauthorized use. Without a definition, there is no baseline against which unauthorized use can be measured. The policy should be specific — 'users may access systems for business purposes' is insufficient; 'users may access CUI repositories using company-issued devices from approved locations, authenticated with MFA' is sufficient.

name: User Behavior Monitoring Records
description: Records from SIEM user behavior analytics, UEBA platforms, EDR user activity monitoring, DLP violation logs, or privileged access management session recordings — showing detection of activity that deviates from the authorized use baseline.
reasoning: Operational evidence for SI.L2-3.14.7[b] — that unauthorized use is actually being identified. The acceptable use policy establishes the definition; monitoring records demonstrate detection capability. Assessors look for both configuration (detection rules exist) and operation (detections are being generated and investigated). Zero unauthorized use detections over a long period in a large organization is suspicious and prompts deeper investigation into whether monitoring is functioning.

name: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) Configuration
description: Configuration of the EDR platform — showing behavioral detection rules, response automation (isolate, kill process, quarantine), telemetry collection scope, threat hunting capability, and integration with SIEM. Distinct from basic AV configuration in depth and capability.
reasoning: Modern EDR platforms simultaneously address SI.L1-3.14.2 (malicious code protection via behavioral detection), SI.L2-3.14.6 (system monitoring for attacks via endpoint telemetry and behavioral analytics), and SI.L2-3.14.7 (unauthorized use detection via process and user activity monitoring). A single EDR deployment and configuration artifact can demonstrate multiple SI practices. Assessors distinguish between basic AV (signature-only, satisfies SI.L1-3.14.2 minimally) and EDR (behavioral + signatures, satisfies the broader monitoring practices).

name: Vulnerability Flaw Identification Records
description: Records demonstrating that system flaws are identified within the required timeframe — vulnerability scan results with scan dates, configuration scan reports, software inventory showing software versions against known vulnerability databases, and automated flaw detection tool outputs.
reasoning: Evidence for SI.L1-3.14.1[a,b] — that identification timeframes are defined and met. The scan date on vulnerability reports demonstrates the identification cadence. Assessors verify that the most recent scan falls within the policy-defined identification window (e.g., monthly scans where policy requires monthly identification). Multiple scan results over time also demonstrate the periodic nature of identification — not a one-time event.

name: Malicious Code Protection Scope Documentation
description: Documentation identifying which system components require malicious code protection and confirming protection is deployed — including workstations, servers, email gateway, web proxy, removable media controls, and any explicitly excluded components with justification.
reasoning: Direct evidence for SI.L1-3.14.2[a] — that designated protection locations are identified. The Assessment Guide requires explicit identification of locations before protection can be confirmed as comprehensive. Scope documentation that lists all required protection points, cross-referenced with the deployment coverage report, gives assessors confidence that coverage is intentional and complete rather than ad hoc.
